title: Greece decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Greece removed criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts between adults in 1951.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Jordan decriminalizes consensual same-sex acts
description: A 1951 revision of Jordan's criminal code made private, non-commercial sex between consenting adults legal regardless of gender, setting the age of consent at 16.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Spring Fire launches the lesbian pulp fiction genre
description: Marijane Meaker, writing under the pen name Vin Packer, published the first lesbian paperback original novel. Spring Fire sold 1.5 million copies and opened the lesbian pulp fiction genre.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dale Jennings fights entrapment charge in Los Angeles
description: Arrested in a police sting at Westlake Park, Dale Jennings contested the solicitation charge with backing from the Mattachine Society. The trial ended in a hung jury, drew national attention, and sharply increased the society's membership.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Christine Jorgensen's transition makes world headlines
description: Christine Jorgensen became the first person whose gender-affirming surgery received broad international press coverage, bringing transgender lives into public view.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay magazine Adonis launches in Japan
description: The Japanese magazine Adonis, aimed at homosexual men, began publication in 1952 with the writer Yukio Mishima among its contributors.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Diana Foundation founded in Houston
description: A small circle of friends in Houston started the Diana Foundation on 19 March 1953. It is regarded as the oldest continuously active gay organization in the United States and stages annual fundraisers including the Diana Awards.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Executive Order 10450 bars gay people from US federal jobs
description: Amid the Lavender Scare that ran alongside McCarthy's anti-communist campaign, President Eisenhower signed an executive order excluding homosexuals from federal employment. Thousands of workers lost their jobs over actual or suspected sexual orientation.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Alan Turing dies after conviction for homosexuality
description: The mathematician and computing pioneer Alan Turing died of cyanide poisoning on 7 June 1954, ruled a suicide. Eighteen months earlier a British court had forced him to choose between prison and hormone treatment as punishment for homosexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: British courts convict Montagu, Pitt-Rivers and Wildeblood
description: In a police campaign against prominent gay men, Lord Montagu, Michael Pitt-Rivers and Peter Wildeblood were convicted of homosexual offences. Public unease over the prosecutions helped prompt the Wolfenden committee's review of the law.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Arcadie, France's first homophile group, is formed
description: Arcadie began in 1954 as the first organized homosexual group in France, giving the country's homophile movement its earliest institutional home.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Daughters of Bilitis founded in San Francisco
description: Four lesbian couples, among them Del Martin and Phyllis Lyon, created the first national lesbian political and social organization in the United States. The group hosted public forums, supported isolated lesbians, and took part in research; its name came from Pierre Louÿs's Bilitis poems.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mattachine Society opens a New York City chapter
description: The homophile Mattachine Society expanded to the East Coast with the founding of its New York City chapter in 1955.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Ladder begins publication
description: The Daughters of Bilitis started The Ladder, the first lesbian periodical distributed nationally in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Thailand decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Thailand removed criminal penalties for consensual homosexual acts in 1956.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Florida's Johns Committee begins targeting homosexuals
description: The Florida Legislative Investigation Committee, known as the Johns Committee, was set up in 1956. After failing to link civil rights groups to communism, it turned to investigating homosexuals as an alleged threat to national security.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Harry Benjamin introduces the term transsexual
description: US physician Harry Benjamin coined the word transsexual in 1957, shaping medical vocabulary around transgender identities for decades.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Wolfenden Report urges Britain to end prosecution of gay men
description: A British government committee recommended that consensual homosexual behaviour between adults in private no longer be a criminal offence. The report laid the groundwork for the 1967 reform in England and Wales.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Evelyn Hooker publishes study on gay men's mental health
description: Psychologist Evelyn Hooker showed that homosexual men were as psychologically well-adjusted as heterosexual men. Her findings became a key argument for the American Psychiatric Association's 1973 removal of homosexuality from its list of disorders.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tom of Finland debuts on the cover of Physique Pictorial
description: The homoerotic artist Tom of Finland appeared for the first time on the cover of the Los Angeles magazine Physique Pictorial, launching his international visibility.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Homosexual Law Reform Society forms in Britain
description: Campaigners founded the Homosexual Law Reform Society in 1958 to press for the decriminalization recommended by the Wolfenden Report.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Barbara Gittings starts New York Daughters of Bilitis chapter
description: Activist Barbara Gittings founded the New York chapter of the lesbian organization Daughters of Bilitis in 1958.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: US Supreme Court sides with ONE magazine in obscenity case
description: In One, Inc. v. Olesen the Supreme Court reversed a ruling that had declared the gay magazine ONE obscene. It was the court's first decision touching homosexuality, extending constitutional free-speech protection to pro-homosexual writing.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gold Coast leather bar opens in Chicago
description: Chuck Renslow and Dom Orejudos opened the Gold Coast in Chicago in 1958, the first gay leather bar in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: ITV airs South, the UK's first gay television drama
description: ITV, then the UK's only national commercial broadcaster, screened South with Peter Wyngarde in 1959 — the country's first television drama with a gay theme.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Cooper Do-nuts uprising in Los Angeles
description: Patrons at the Cooper Do-nuts cafe in Los Angeles resisted police harassment in 1959, with rioters arrested by the LAPD. The incident is cited as the first homosexual uprising in the United States, though details are debated by historians.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Air Force reservists win challenge to anti-gay discharges
description: Fannie Mae Clackum and Grace Garner became the first people to successfully contest their discharges from the US military for alleged homosexuality. The court's decision rested on the lack of evidence that the two women were lesbians.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Executive Order 10450 used to bar transgender military service
description: Scholars date the application of Executive Order 10450 to exclude transgender people from the US armed forces to at least 1960.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Hungary decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Hungary removed criminal penalties for consensual sodomy between adults in 1961.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Victim premieres, first English-language film to say homosexual
description: The British film Victim, starring Dirk Bogarde alongside a blackmail plot about gay men, opened on 31 August 1961 as the first English-language feature to use the word homosexual.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Vatican bars men with homosexual inclinations from ordination
description: The Vatican declared in 1961 that men drawn to homosexuality should be excluded from religious vows and ordination in the Roman Catholic Church.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Rejected airs as first US TV documentary on homosexuality
description: KQED in San Francisco broadcast The Rejected on 11 September 1961, the first documentary about homosexuality shown on American television.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: José Sarria runs for San Francisco Board of Supervisors
description: Drag performer José Sarria became the first openly gay candidate for public office in the United States with his 1961 run for the San Francisco Board of Supervisors.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Illinois becomes first US state to decriminalize sodomy
description: By adopting the American Law Institute's Model Penal Code, Illinois removed sodomy from its criminal law, the first US state to do so. Private consensual sex went unpunished, though the new code criminalized public lewdness.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Czechoslovakia decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Czechoslovakia, covering today's Czechia as well as Slovakia, removed criminal penalties for consensual sodomy between adults in 1962.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Tavern Guild forms as first US gay business association
description: Gay bar owners in San Francisco created the Tavern Guild in 1962 in response to ongoing police harassment and bar closures, including the Tay-Bush Inn raid. It operated until 1995.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Dorian Society founded as New Zealand's first gay organization
description: The Dorian Society, which existed until 1988, was set up in 1962 as the first organization for gay men in New Zealand.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: East Coast Homophile Organizations forms in Philadelphia
description: Regional chapters of the Daughters of Bilitis, the Janus Society and the Mattachine Society established ECHO in Philadelphia in 1963 as an East Coast umbrella for the homophile movement.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Israeli ruling halts enforcement of sodomy ban
description: A 1963 judicial decision directed that the sodomy provision of the British Mandate-era law of 1936, which had in practice gone unenforced, should not be applied. Sexual acts between men were thereby de facto decriminalized in Israel.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: ASK and Canada's first gay periodicals appear
description: Canada's first gay-positive organization, the Association for Social Knowledge, formed in Vancouver in 1964, alongside the country's first gay periodicals: the ASK Newsletter in Vancouver and Gay from Toronto. Gay, the first periodical with the word in its title, grew quickly as Gay International, soon joined by Two.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Ted Northe founds the Imperial Court of Canada
description: In March 1964 Ted Northe created the Imperial Court of Canada, a monarchist society built largely around drag performers that became a driving force for equality. Its network grew to more than 14 chapters and ranks as Canada's oldest continuously running LGBT organization.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Ladder puts lesbians on its cover
description: The September 1964 issue of The Ladder carried the first cover photograph of lesbians, showing two women from behind on a beach looking out to sea.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Life magazine reports on gay life in America
description: Paul Welch's June 1964 Life article on homosexuality in America marked the first time a national US publication covered gay issues.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Council on Religion and the Homosexual forms
description: Founded in 1964, the Council on Religion and the Homosexual brought clergy and activists together and was the first US group to carry the word homosexual in its name.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Politicians face questions at first gay-vote event
description: The Council on Religion and the Homosexual hosted a 1965 forum where local politicians answered questions on issues affecting gay and lesbian people, including police intimidation. It is considered the first recorded courting of the gay vote.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Everett Klippert jailed indefinitely for homosexuality
description: Everett George Klippert, the last person imprisoned for homosexuality in Canada, was arrested in 1965 for private consensual sex with men. Declared incurably homosexual, he received indefinite preventive detention as a dangerous sexual offender — a case whose perceived cruelty fueled the reform passed in 1969.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First Annual Reminder picket at Independence Hall
description: On 4 July 1965 conservatively dressed gay men and lesbians picketed outside Independence Hall in Philadelphia. The demonstration opened the series of Annual Reminders held each year through 1969.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Vanguard youth organization forms in the Tenderloin
description: LGBT youth in San Francisco's low-income Tenderloin district created Vanguard in 1965, regarded as the first gay liberation organization in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Mattachine Sip-In challenges bar service ban at Julius'
description: Members of the Mattachine Society staged a Sip-In at Julius Bar in New York City, confronting the State Liquor Authority practice of refusing alcohol service to gay customers.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: North American homophile groups form national conference
description: The National Planning Conference of Homophile Organizations was established in 1966, becoming the North American Conference of Homophile Organizations (NACHO) later that year.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Compton's Cafeteria riot in San Francisco
description: In August 1966 transgender women and Vanguard members fought back against police harassment at Compton's Cafeteria in San Francisco's Tenderloin. It stands among the first recorded transgender riots in US history, three years before Stonewall.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Lilli Vincenz shows her face on The Ladder's cover
description: The January 1966 issue of The Ladder carried the first cover photograph of a lesbian with her face visible: activist Lilli Vincenz.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Los Angeles motorcade protests military exclusion
description: For Armed Forces Day 1966, a coalition of homophile groups demonstrated against the exclusion of LGBT people from the US armed services. The Los Angeles group's 15-car motorcade has been described as the nation's first gay pride parade.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: First US gay and lesbian community center opens
description: The Society for Individual Rights opened America's first gay and lesbian community center in San Francisco in 1966.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Phoenix magazine launches in Kansas City
description: The Phoenix Society for Individual Freedom in Kansas City published The Phoenix: Midwest Homophile Voice, the first LGBT magazine in the American Midwest.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Sexual Offences Act decriminalizes gay sex in England and Wales
description: The Sexual Offences Act 1967 ended criminal liability for private consensual sex between men over 21 in England and Wales. The act covered neither Scotland nor Northern Ireland nor the Channel Islands.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Nuestro Mundo, first gay rights group in Latin America, forms
description: Grupo Nuestro Mundo formed in Greater Buenos Aires as the first gay rights organization in Argentina — and in Latin America as a whole. Some accounts date the founding to 1969.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Black Cat Tavern raid sparks protests in Los Angeles
description: Twelve plainclothes officers raided the Black Cat Tavern in Silver Lake on New Year's Day 1967, beating and arresting staff and patrons. P.R.I.D.E. organized protests from 5 January — the first use of the word Pride later tied to the LGBT rights movement — which energized homosexual rights activity.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Los Angeles Advocate publishes its first issue
description: The Advocate started in September 1967 as The Los Angeles Advocate, a local newsletter warning gay men about police raids on the city's gay bars. It later grew into a national LGBT news magazine.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Study treats male homosexuality as a fact of life
description: Wainwright Churchill's book Homosexual Behavior Among Males approached homosexuality scientifically as a normal variation. It introduced the term homoerotophobia, seen as a possible forerunner of the word homophobia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Oscar Wilde Bookshop opens in New York City
description: The Oscar Wilde Bookshop opened in New York City in 1967 as the world's first bookstore dedicated to homosexual-oriented literature.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Columbia recognizes first US gay student group
description: The Student Homophile League at Columbia University became the first gay student organization in the United States to gain official institutional recognition.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: CBS airs network documentary The Homosexuals
description: The 1967 CBS Reports episode The Homosexuals was the first US network television documentary to deal with homosexuality.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: East Germany decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: East Germany's 1968 penal code reform ended the criminalization of consensual same-sex acts between adults under Paragraph 175.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Bulgaria decriminalizes homosexual acts
description: Bulgaria removed criminal penalties for consensual same-sex acts between adults in 1968.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: National Transsexual Counseling Unit begins work
description: The National Transsexual Counseling Unit emerged in 1968 from the network of transgender social, psychological and medical support services built after the Compton's Cafeteria riot. It was the first peer-run transgender support and advocacy organization in the world.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Patch patrons answer police raid with flowers
description: After police arrested two patrons in a raid on the Los Angeles bar The Patch, owner Lee Glaze rallied the crowd, bought out a nearby flower shop, and led a caravan to the police station, where the demonstrators decorated it with flowers and bailed out the arrested men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Canada decriminalizes homosexual acts through Bill C-150
description: Bill C-150 removed criminal penalties for private consensual same-sex acts between adults in Canada in 1969. Prime Minister Pierre Trudeau famously argued that the state had no business in the nation's bedrooms.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Poland removes penalties for homosexual prostitution
description: Poland's 1969 penal code reform ended the criminalization of homosexual prostitution; consensual same-sex acts themselves had been legal since 1932.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Daughters of Bilitis chapter forms in Melbourne
description: An Australian arm of the Daughters of Bilitis formed in Melbourne in 1969 and is now considered the first homosexual rights organization in Australia.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Gay Liberation Front forms in the United States
description: The Gay Liberation Front took shape in America in 1969 in the wake of the Stonewall riots, becoming the first gay organization to carry the word gay in its name.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Stonewall riots begin in New York City
description: Patrons of the Stonewall Inn in Greenwich Village resisted a police raid on 28 June 1969, setting off several days of unrest. The riots became the catalyst of the modern gay liberation movement worldwide.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: West Germany eases Paragraph 175
description: A 1969 reform in West Germany relaxed Paragraph 175, ending the criminalization of consensual sex between adult men.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: The Cockettes debut at the Palace Theatre
description: The drag performance troupe the Cockettes gave their first show on 31 December 1969 at the Palace Theatre in San Francisco's North Beach.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: Campaign Against Moral Persecution launches in Australia
description: CAMP, the Campaign Against Moral Persecution, formed in Australia in 1970 to push for homosexual law reform and public visibility.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)

title: American librarians form the Task Force on Gay Liberation
description: Founded in 1970 within the American Library Association, the Task Force on Gay Liberation — today the Rainbow Round Table — is the oldest LGBTQ professional organization in the United States.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Timeline of LGBTQ history, 20th century (CC BY-SA 4.0)
